So I, all the way back, I started a career when I was at IBM, and I built the first content hub outside of IBM.com called SecuredIntelligence.com, and this was before content marketing was even a term, you know, and it lasted 15 years, just got folded back this year, but it really kind of proved out for me that the best kind of marketing is not doing marketing.
It's really just becoming a media company.
And...
Brands should act more like publishers, and that's how you build trust and earn your audience and then build distribution and all that, right?
So pretty much my entire career was doing that for brands, except I would get really bored if I was only doing content and I wanted to really kind of be more widespread.
And so I did that at HP.
I built a contributor network of over 1,000 contributors, grew to over a million visitors a month.

And the whole thing was like going to conferences, finding experts on like really technical topics, and then poaching really good editors from like Wired and Computer World, and then creating this publishing engine.

Yeah, so we think of it as like we're never done and we think of content as an asset and the half-life of that asset is getting shorter, aka it's decaying faster because of AI visibility and because things are changing faster.
And so like if you think of like pages in an e-commerce catalog, you don't think of them as just pages and you publish and you're done.
Like Amazon spends over a billion a year in enriching their catalog, deduping, and the catalog is what drives transactions and conversions.
And so like I think things are going to shift to where it's not just like the page itself.
Like content is your asset and it's how you build trust.
